speaker: interviewer
text: So please continue, then, where you had left off from.

speaker: interviewee
text: I mentioned that--

speaker: interviewer
text: The way they concealed the footprints. The ingenious use of the sieve, and the snow in the sieve, yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right. So Dora's father and her brother were both killed on one of the journeys to go out to get food, and that's why Dora, my future wife, became the sole person that was able to go out between those local population and acquire-- acquire food.

speaker: interviewer
text: Because she couldn't-- she couldn't be identified as a Jewess.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] There are a couple of things I would like to go back to that I forgot to mention. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I did mention to you that my brother [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] was killed at the same time with my mother, my brother-in-law, my sister, and their child.

speaker: interviewer
text: [NON-ENGLISH ]

speaker: interviewee
text: [NON-ENGLISH] [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] My brother was in camp Sasow. I was, at the time, in the [GERMAN] in Zloczow. He got typhoid, [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] and he was brought to the hospital, which was in the vicinity of the [GERMAN] in Zloczow. After he got a- slightly better, I was able to bring him some food, and paid off someone to let him go, instead of back-- instead of back to Sasow, to the camp, to my mother in the ghetto Zloczow for him to [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] be able to come back to-- to his health. That was a few days before the action in Zloczow, the liquidation of the Zloczow ghetto. And I still, up till today, live with [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] guilt that I was instrumental in him going to ghetto instead of going back to the camp, where he was killed with my mother, together. And I keep thinking, constantly, that maybe if he had gotten to the camp in Sasow, back, maybe he would have survived this-- the-- the war-- the war, like I was lucky to do. So it's it's something that-- that comes to my mind often. Also, I'd like to mention to you that Sasow is known of its own rebellion. Sasow camp, when they saw they were bringing-- Sasow camp was located in a estate. [PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS] There was a paper factory in Sasow, years back, and the owner of that factory lived in that estate where the-- where the [NON-ENGLISH] was situated. When they surrounded the camp in Sasow, they took individuals and they brought them into the estate, into the building, and they were shooting them there. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Some people in the Sasow camp rebelled. They called, [NON-ENGLISH], and they started running in all directions. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Some 50-- over 50 people succeeded in running through the barbed wires into the woods. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Many of them were killed in the woods, and I know of 17 that succeeded in saving their lives. They ran away in the area of the same woods that I was hiding in. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Some of them are caught-- were caught [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] because the-- the front line between the Soviet Union and Germany changed.

speaker: interviewer
text: So one day, the-- the Soviets were in that part of the woods, and the next day, the Germans came back. And they were caught in-- in-- in one of those fights, and some of them got killed. But some of them survived, and they survived only because they had the courage to run away.

speaker: interviewee
text: There's one more thing I'd like to mention that I forgot to mention before. As-- while I was in Janowska, I always had a gun, and I concealed that gun in a quart paint-- in a quart-- an empty quart paint can. In an empty can from paint-- a quart-- put it in and dumped the quart can into a gallon of paint, and this is how I had a gun constantly.

speaker: interviewer
text: It was a hand gun?

speaker: interviewee
text: A hand gun. A pistol. A small gun.

speaker: interviewer
text: What type was it? Do you recall?

speaker: interviewee
text: I do not have a recollection. I wasn't good with guns in-- in the army, and I wasn't good in guns later on.

speaker: interviewer
text: You never fired this weapon, then?

speaker: interviewee
text: Never fired that-- that-- that-- that thing. [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] OK. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I lived-- I was liberated [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] in March of 1943-- the end of March, 1943, by the Russians.

speaker: interviewer
text: Now you say 43. My feeling is it must've been 44.

speaker: interviewer
text: Really? Because the Russians didn't reach Warsaw until '44.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. 194-- March, '40-- in the end of March, 1944.

speaker: interviewer
text: It was '44, then.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: We were liberated by the Russians.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: Now, our bunker that we were hiding in was found by the Russians. I gotta tell you, also, that it was not a-- a good time, because there was a drunken lieutenant-- a Russian lieutenant-- that wanted to kill us out-- all of them, immediately. But there was also a Jewish officer between-- within-- within that group that stopped him from killing us, and he advised us to go back-- back from the front line, to disappear going out. We didn't wait. We lost no time, and we immediately went back to Podkamien. And from Podkamien to [NON-ENGLISH], and in [NON-ENGLISH], we settled down for a while. The [?Szarf? ] family started settling down. The young people were taken to the army. I was drafted to work [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] in [? Voyenstroy. ?] That was a Russian organization that supplied the army with food, with vegetables, with fruits, and other things--

speaker: interviewer
text: This was near [NON-ENGLISH], then?

speaker: interviewee
text: That-- that was in Rowne.

speaker: interviewer
text: Rowne. That's-- oh, that's in the Ukraine already.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, in Rowne.

speaker: interviewer
text: East of Lwow. East of Lwow.

speaker: interviewee
text: East of Lwow. And I worked there and did the same thing I was doing previously, painting portraits of the Russian big shots.

speaker: interviewer
text: You were in uniform.

speaker: interviewee
text: I was not in uniform. I was free. And the Germans were bombing Rowne practically every night, and every night we were hiding. We knew like clockwork that around 9:00, 10:00 in the night, the-- the planes will come over Rowne and dump some bombs. And one morning when I went to work in [? Voyenstroy, ?] a school was hit right across the street. And I was walking in the street, and the whole building was up in the air and set down like a pancake.

speaker: interviewer
text: You saw that.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I was watching this. And every morning, after a night of bombing, we kept going into town and see what-- what else was hit. The front lines moved back towards Poland. And at that time, a decree came that all the former Polish citizens can return to their hometowns. And I went to Dubno, and from there I went back to Zloczow. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] By-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the family [?Szarf?] moved from [NON-ENGLISH] back to Zloczow, so I found myself back with family. From there, I went to Lemberg, to Lwow, to Zezow, and from Zezow to Cracow. I stayed in Cracow a few weeks, and we started black market-- marketeering. People were bringing in food, sugar, on truckloads that they were buying from the Russians, and selling them in Cracow.

speaker: interviewer
text: This was at the end of 1944.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right. Not in the end. It was in the beginning-- in the beginning, in May of 1944.

speaker: interviewer
text: May. OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: Later on, I moved back to Zezow, and established a home for my future wife and her family. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] We were married in Zezow [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] April 30, 1945.

speaker: interviewer
text: The war was still on then.

speaker: interviewee
text: The war was on, but not-- not in that area. The war with Germany ended in 1945.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes. Not till May, though.

speaker: interviewee
text: May of '45.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: A couple days after our marriage, we left Zezow. [PAUSES FOR 14 SECONDS] I'm trying to give you the information. [PAUSES FOR 15 SECONDS] Left Zezow and went to Kosice, in Czechoslovakia. From Kosice, we went to [NON-ENGLISH] Mare, in Romania-- in Romania. From there, I went back to Bratislava, Czechoslovakia. We went to Budapest, Hungary. From Hungary, I went to Graz in Austria, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and from Austria, I went-- was shipped [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] with a truck-- open truck to Italy. The first stop was Udine. From Udine, I went to Treviso. From Treviso, to Modena, where we stayed in a military academy. From there, we were transferred to a camp in Santa Marina de Leuca, south-- south Italy, very south Italy. Marina de Leuca was a showplace for Mussolini. There's a-- he erected a monument there, and there were many, many villas, unoccupied, and we were settled there, in Marina de Leuca. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We were given food and clothing by the UNRRA, United Nations organizations for the-- relief organization. And we had practically everything we needed. There were several hundred people in that area. They opened a school of-- to teach English, and Dora went there to teach English-- to learn the language. From Marina de Leuca, we wound up in Bari. Bari was a large camp-- established camp. And the survivors created drama clubs, soccer team, boxing teams-- all kinds of sports. There was an organizational [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] committee, and we were provided with most of our needs.

speaker: interviewer
text: Bari, as I recall, an Italian port, was very badly damaged by an ammunition ship explosion. Did you see any evidences of that?

speaker: interviewee
text: I-- I saw no evidence of that. We were a few kilometers out of town-- out of town of Bari. But I bought a Friedlander camera for about a few dollars-- $5-- and I were going every day to the beaches in Bari and taking pictures of--

speaker: interviewer
text: Holiday makers.

speaker: interviewee
text: Holiday makers. And developed them during the night with a young man as a partner by the name of Irving [? Mala, ?] and bring those pictures back to the beaches the next day and make money. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We also were involved in some black marketeering, like the American soldiers [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] could not get rid of single dollars. They wanted large-- larger--

speaker: interviewer
text: Large bills.

speaker: interviewee
text: So they were selling those bills, those single-dollar bills and smaller denominations, for less than-- than big money. And we used to take that-- ride on a open train to Milano from Bari and sell it there for a few cents more. And this is how we acquired some money. Now, my traveling to Italy was intended to go to Palestine. The political situation there was such, again, that the British closed the borders and opened a camp in-- in Cyprus. And at that time, I decided that I'm not going to another camp, and I wrote a letter to my uncle-- my father's brother-- in the United States to send me papers. He sent me papers from HIAS-- through the HIAS-- and I applied to the American Embassy in Naples to come to America. It took a long time until I received my permits to come to the United States. In that respect, I was also lucky, because I was born in Czechoslovakia, and the-- the Czech quota was much larger than the people that were available from Czechoslovakia. My wife, Dora, was not able to get a certificate to come to the United States because she was born in Poland. But I refused to accept mine until she comes along. So in November of 1947, I got my certificate with my wife to come to the United States.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you were waiting, really, about two and a half years--

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No. I waited 22 months.

speaker: interviewer
text: From liberation.

speaker: interviewee
text: Not from liberation. From-- from the day I arrived in Italy. I spent 22 months in Italy waiting to get to a permanent place to--

speaker: interviewer
text: And even longer from the time you were liberated.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes, I-- I was liberated, as I told you, in-- in 1944.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I came to the United States. I was-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I lived with my family for a few months on Dumont Avenue in Brooklyn. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I gotta tell you that my times in the United States were not easy. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] First of all, I knew no English. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I went to a night school in Brooklyn, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] and I couldn't find a job. My brother-in-law was in the oil business, and he rented some space for his trucks-- for the oil trucks-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] on Dobbin Street in Brooklyn-- in Brooklyn, Greenpoint. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I was able to get a job in-- in a body-building place. Let me go back a little bit. I was first taken to a company by the name of Metropolitan Distributors. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] They were very anxious to have me as an artist-- as a sign painter. But there was nobody to talk to me. Nobody was able to communicate with me. So my brother-in-law found a job for me in a place where they spoke Italian, they spoke Polish, they spoke German. Most of these languages I knew. And I worked there. By-- even here in the United States, in the beginning I had problems. I came to work with a Yiddish newspaper, and they were tearing up my newspaper, and they were making all kinds of antisemitic remarks. One guy, at one time, told me he sorry that Hitler did not kill me, and so forth. I got violently angry at him and threw a big hammer at him, and he became my very best friend since then. After I showed him that I am not afraid of him, he became a friend. After 22 months-- 22 months there, yeah-- the shop closed, for whatever reasons. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And at that time, I knew, already, English, and I went back to Metropolitan Distributors, where they hired me. Metropolitan Distributors became the Hertz Corporation. The Hertz Corporation was sold to RCA, and RCA, at the end of my stay there was sold to Penske. And I spent 34 years at that job, in the beginning only in the lettering department. I became the head of the lettering department. Now, this organization was renting trucks, and they needed somebody to do the art work on trucks. That was my profession here in the United States. We did all the liquor stores, all the-- the milk trucks, bakery trucks, everything. Thousands of trucks were rented out, and I was doing all the lettering. I developed a department, and I hired mostly European people to do the sign painting. And they were very happy with me. It was a beautiful company to work for. They made it easy for me to buy a house, and I was treated very well. They also recognized my ability, and in a little while, they made me the head of the whole department-- painting, lettering-- and I had, under my supervision, 23 people. I was very well treated there.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you became a senior executive, then.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I was-- I was not permitted to join the union. In the beginning, I was a union member. But later, I became an executive, and I was--

speaker: interviewer
text: You were part of management.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right, part of management, and I worked there for 34 years. And I retired from that company. Thank God, [INTERPOSING VOICES] I--

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you retire?

speaker: interviewee
text: I retired in 1982, the beginning of 1982, and I came to Florida after 34 years with--

speaker: interviewer
text: So you retired when you were about 64.

speaker: interviewee
text: 64 and a half. You counted quickly. I was 64 and a half, because my wife [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] sold her business. She owned a nursery school for 31 years, and she did not want to spend another winter in the cold climate, and we came and settled in Florida. I got to tell you-- got to go back again. On my third anniversary in the United States, my wife, Dora, had a cerebral hemorrhage and passed away, leaving me with a seven-week-old child. So when I mentioned before that life for me in the United States was not all too good in the beginning, it ended well, but it was a very tough time for me. [PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS] We live now in Boynton Beach. I am financially secure. My wife is busy with activities in the community.

speaker: interviewer
text: Well, you were left with a seven-month-old daughter, Marsha.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right. Marsha is now-- she was born in 1950-- she's now married, going for her doctorate, and very well-established.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you raise her, though, by yourself?

speaker: interviewee
text: Not easily. I had a relative that lived in Brooklyn that moved in with me to my apartment, and they took care of the child. Then I got married, and my wife took care of it.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes. Well, there was a period of years-- let's see. It would be a period of years of about-- [PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS] well, about 10 years, almost--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. And--

speaker: interviewer
text: During which this relative helped you to raise Marsha.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's right. That's correct.

speaker: interviewer
text: And since your retirement here in Florida, how have you spent your time?

speaker: interviewee
text: Very well. Much better than in the past. I'm now--

speaker: interviewer
text: How have you occupied yourself?

speaker: interviewee
text: Right now? Well, I'm helpful in the community. I play tennis and I play golf, and I look forward to every morning when I get up and do my-- my pleasures.

speaker: interviewer
text: Every day is Sunday.

speaker: interviewee
text: Every day is Sunday.

speaker: interviewer
text: Or Shabbat.

speaker: interviewee
text: That's right. Well, I-- my children come to visit me. My grandchildren come to visit us, and we go to visit them.

speaker: interviewer
text: How many grandchildren do you have?

speaker: interviewee
text: Five.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tell me their names and ages.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK. Adam is the oldest. He's 20. He is in college. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Then comes Julie, 16. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Then is Brian, 10, Rebecca, six, and Jordan, six.

speaker: interviewer
text: And these grandchildren--

speaker: interviewee
text: Three children. From three children.

speaker: interviewer
text: These grandchildren are from Marsha?

speaker: interviewee
text: Marsha has no children.

speaker: interviewer
text: No children. These are from your--

speaker: interviewee
text: My wife-- my wife had two daughters, and they are married, and they  had the grandchildren.

speaker: interviewer
text: So these are really-- really, in a sense, step-grandchildren.

speaker: interviewee
text: We consider them-- I consider them my grandchildren.

speaker: interviewer
text: Of course they are-- they-- you consider them your grandchildren. But I'm just saying, for the record, really, they are--

speaker: interviewee
text: For the record, they are my step-grandchildren.

speaker: interviewer
text: Step-grandchildren, yes. [PAUSES FOR 11 SECONDS] What message do you think you would want to convey to the world? [PAUSES FOR 12 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: Israel, in my opinion, was created because of the guilt of the world. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] They-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] they needed some atonement for what the non-Jews did to us. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Israel-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Israel is of great importance to Jewish people. We have a home now. If we find ourselves threatened, like [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] in Bosnia-Herzegovina recently, or in Ethiopia, where Jews were threatened, they-- Israelis found a way of taking them out of the danger and bringing them to Israel. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] My message would be that Jews could not rely of justice from the world. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Poland now is riddled with antisemitism, even though there are only over 10,000 Jews living there, as far as I know. I do not trust the Germans to feel remorse for what they did to us. Jews have to rely on Jews. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I pray to God [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] that Israel will be strong enough [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] to be able to safe-- to save her existence for the people that live there now, and for the people around the world.
